coredata.dc:title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and application for non-enzymatic detection of glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wenfang
Jia, Haishuang
Zhang, Jie
Tang, Jianhe
Wang, Jun
Fang, Dawei
coredata.dc:description: A hydrothermal method was used for the preparation four kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) by using citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA), urea (U), melamine (MA) and ammonia solution (AS) as the nitrogen source, respectively. The NCQDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR and XPS, and the optical properties were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. It is found that the NCQDs(AS) from ammonia solution (AS) as nitrogen source are uniform size spherical nanoparticles and have prominent optical properties. Influencing factors including solution acidity, interfering substances, Ag+ ions concentration and glucose concentration on the detection of glucose are taken into account. In the presence of Ag+ ions, the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution was severely quenched due to the surface energy transfer from NCQDs(AS) to Ag+ ions. In weakly alkaline condition, the detected glucose is oxidized to gluconic acid and Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag in aqueous solution at 60 °C. Thereby, the abscission of Ag+ ions from the surface of NCQDs(AS) results in the fluorescence recovery of NCQDs(AS). The glucose can be analyzed quantitatively by NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions with a wide linear region (0.1 μM–1000 μM) and low limit of detection (LOD) (0.375 μM). Also, the selectivity and anti-interference ability experiment of the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions for glucose detection were discussed. In addition, cytotoxicity and cell imaging experiments of NCQDs(AS) were researched. Overall, the prepared NCQDs(AS) are expected to be widely used in different biological research fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Ag+ ions
Non-enzyme glucose detection
Fluorescence
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Glucose of detection in actual samples
TEM, FT-IR and XPS of prepared NCQDs
UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of prepared NCQDs in the presence of Ag+ ions
Effects of pH values, NaCl concentration and light irradiation time on the fluorescence of NCQDs in aqueous solution
Effect of Ag+ ions concentration on the fluorescence spectrum of NCQDs(AS)
Effect of solution acidity and glucose concentration on fluorescence spectra of NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions for glucose detection
Principle and mechanism of glucose detection by using NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescent probe in the presence of Ag+ ions
Glucose detection in real sample analyses
Cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging of NCQDs(AS)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Department of Education of Liaoning Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (21371084), and the Innovative Team Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province (LT2018018) of China. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participating in this work.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation ( 21371084 ), and the Innovative Team Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province ( LT2018018 ) of China. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participating in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (1), HR-TEM images (2) and size distribution (3) of (a) NCQDs(EDA), (b) NCQDs(U), (c) NCQDs(MA) and (d) NCQDs(AS).
FT-IR spectra of (a) NCQDs(EDA), (b) NCQDs(U), (c) NCQDs(MA) and (d) NCQDs(AS).
Full scan XPS spectrum of (a-1) NCQDs(EDA), (b-1) NCQDs(U), (c-1) NCQDs(MA) and (d-1) NCQDs(AS).

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra spectra of (a) NCQDs(EDA), (b) NCQDs(U), (c) NCQDs(MA) and (d) NCQDs(AS). Comparison of fluorescence decay profiles of NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS) (e).
UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of (a) NCQDs(EDA), (b) NCQDs(U), (c) NCQDs(MA) and (d) NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions and glucose.
Effects of solution acidity (pH value) (a), NaCl concentration (b) and light irradiation time (c) on the fluorescence spectra (λex = 340 nm) of the prepared NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution.

Fluorescence spectra (λex = 340 nm) (a) of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in Ag+ ions concentration range from 0.0 μM to 3000 μM, plots (b) of the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) and Ag+ ion concentrations (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs(AS) solution in the absence and presence of Ag+ ions, respectively) and photograph (c) of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the absence and presence of Ag+ ions under visible light (c-1) and ultraviolet light (c-2).

Effects of solution acidity (pH) (a-1) and glucose concentration (b-1) in the rang of 0–5000 μM on the fluorescent spectra (λex = 340 nm) of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions, as well as the corresponding histogram (λem = 441 nm) (a-2) and plot (b-2) of fluorescence enhancement vs concentration of glucose (λem = 441 nm).

The selectivity investigation of the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions for the detection of glucose (λex = 340 nm and λem = 441 nm). The concentration of glucose was 3500 μM, and other interfering substances (1: NaCl, 2: KCl, 3: AA, 4: UA, 5: OA, 6: His, 7: Cre, 8: Fe3+, 9: Cu2+) concentration were the same with glucose.

The interference investigation of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions for the detection of glucose (λex = 340 nm and λem = 441 nm). The concentration of glucose was 3500 μM, and other interfering substances (1: glucose + NaCl, 2: glucose + KCl, 3: glucose + AA, 4: glucose + UA, 5: glucose + OA, 6: glucose + His, 7: glucose + Cre, 8: glucose + Fe3+, 9: glucose + Cu2+) concentration were the same with glucose.
Mechanism of glucose detection using NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescent probe in the presence of Ag+ ions.
Cellular cytotoxicity of NCQDs(AS) towards HeLa cells evaluated by using the MTT assay.
Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with NCQDs(AS) under white field (a), excitations at 405 nm (b) and 488 nm (c) and overlay image (d) of (b) and (c) (10 μm scale bar).
Preparation process of four kinds nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs).
Comparison of experimental results obtained using different glucose detection methods.
Determination of glucose in human urine samples by using the proposed method (Ag+-NCQDs(AS)), glucometer and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and application for non-enzymatic detection of glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Baotou Teachers’ College, Baotou 014030, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Baotou Teachers’ College, Baotou 014030, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to develop a non-enzymatic detection method of glucose, a new fluorescent strategy for detecting glucose is presented based on the Ag+-(NCQDs-NH2)2-OH in aqueous solution, which is similar to the silver ammonia [Ag(NH3)2]OH solution, were formed through the coordination between the –NH2 on the surface of NCQDs(AS) and Ag+ ions under weakly alkaline condition. Upon excitation at 340 nm, the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution at 441 nm emits strong fluorescence. After adding the Ag+ ions, the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution is quenched obviously owing to the energy transfer from NCQDs(AS) to Ag+ ions. Then, under weakly alkaline condition, the glucose is oxidized to gluconic acid and Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag without the glucose oxidase. In the end, Ag+ ions break away the surface of NCQDs(AS), resulting in the fluorescence recovery of NCQDs(AS). As a non-enzymatic glucose probe, the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions can be applied to detect low concentration of glucose.

A hydrothermal method was used for the preparation four kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) by using citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA), urea (U), melamine (MA) and ammonia solution (AS) as the nitrogen source, respectively. The NCQDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR and XPS, and the optical properties were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. It is found that the NCQDs(AS) from ammonia solution (AS) as nitrogen source are uniform size spherical nanoparticles and have prominent optical properties. Influencing factors including solution acidity, interfering substances, Ag+ ions concentration and glucose concentration on the detection of glucose are taken into account. In the presence of Ag+ ions, the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution was severely quenched due to the surface energy transfer from NCQDs(AS) to Ag+ ions. In weakly alkaline condition, the detected glucose is oxidized to gluconic acid and Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag in aqueous solution at 60 °C. Thereby, the abscission of Ag+ ions from the surface of NCQDs(AS) results in the fluorescence recovery of NCQDs(AS). The glucose can be analyzed quantitatively by NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions with a wide linear region (0.1 μM–1000 μM) and low limit of detection (LOD) (0.375 μM). Also, the selectivity and anti-interference ability experiment of the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions for glucose detection were discussed. In addition, cytotoxicity and cell imaging experiments of NCQDs(AS) were researched. Overall, the prepared NCQDs(AS) are expected to be widely used in different biological research fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose plays an important role in the biological body. It is the energy source of living cells and the intermediate product of metabolism. Glucose concentration is an important indicator of the health of people. Abnormal glucose levels increase the risk of various diseases such as hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, high blood pressure and cardiovascular disease [1,2]. Hyperglycemia can be caused by defective insulin secretion or impaired biological effects, while the diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disease characterized by hyperglycemia. It is a common chronic non-communicable disease with serious complications, for example, peripheral neuropathy, arterial disease and various tissue dysfunction and so on [3]. These complications will seriously affect people's normal life and even threaten people's health [4]. Therefore, the early detection of glucose is of great significance for the care and management of diabetes mellitus.

In recent years, many analytical methods have been reported for precise analysis of glucose, including electrochemistry [5,6], enzymatic method [7,8], colorimetry [9,10] and mass spectrometry [11]. However, in many cases, the interference of electrochemically active species [12], existence conditions and related activity and stability of enzym [13-15], high experimental cost and complicated instrument need to be considered. Therefore, a convenient, rapid and economical fluorescence method for detecting glucose is still required. Compared with these methods, non-enzymatic glucose sensing by the optical methods using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has attracted considerable attention [9,16]. CQDs as a novel fluorescent nanomaterials have been widely studied and applied in optoelectronics, photocatalysis, medical diagnosis and biosensors, due to superior optical properties, good chemical stability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [17-21]. In addition, the nitrogen doping can effectively regulate the electronic and chemical characteristics of CQDs to improve the fluorescence properties of CQDs [22-25].

As is known to all, the silver mirror reaction is a classic chemical reaction. The silver ammonia complex can be reduced to metallic silver, and the obtained silver is attached to the wall forming a silver-mirror. Silver mirror reaction is a qualitative experiment for the determination of the aldehyde group and reducing sugar, such as glucose. Therefore, the glucose can be recognized based on the oxidation of aldehyde group. In present paper, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with silver ions are introduced to detect glucose. With the addition of Ag+ ions and glucose in sequence, NCQDs presents an interesting “on-off-on” pattern, realizing the detection of trace amount glucose in the human body. Firstly, after the addition of Ag+ ions, the fluorescence of NCQDs in aqueous solution is quenched obviously, owing to the surface energy transfer from NCQDs to Ag+ ions [7,26]. Secondly, Ag+-(NCQDs-NH2)2-OH in aqueous solution, which is similar to the silver ammonia [Ag(NH3)2]OH solution, were formed through the coordination between the –NH2 on the surface of NCQDs and Ag+ ions under weakly alkaline condition. In the absence of glucose oxidase, glucose is oxidized to gluconic acid and Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag. In the end, Ag+ ions are removed from the surface of NCQDs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence of NCQDs.

In this work, four kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis by using citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA), urea (U), melamine (MA) and ammonia solution (AS) as the nitrogen source, respectively. Under the same external conditions, the NCQDs(AS) has a much higher sensitivity to glucose than other NCQDs. Therefore, the NCQDs(AS) were used as the fluorescence probe in the presence of Ag+ ions for detecting glucose. Under optimum conditions, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions displays a wide linear range with glucose concentrations from 0.1 μM–1000 μM and a lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.375 μM. Importantly, the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions can detect glucose without the influence of interfering substances in the human body. In addition, the cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging of NCQDs(AS) were also studied by using HeLa cells. Therefore, a simple and sensitive strategy for the glucose detection under non-enzymatic condition can be developed based on the oxidation reaction of glucose by using NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescent probe in the presence of Ag+ ions.

All reagents are obtained from distributors (analytical purity, Shanghai Aladdin biochemical technology, China). The water used in the experiment is ultra-pure (18.3 MΩ.cm, Hitech-science tool, Shanghai, China). The HeLa cells were purchased from the Shanghai cell bank of Chinese academy of sciences. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, Talos F200X, USA FEI). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos UK). Fourier Transform-Infrared spectra (FT-IR, IRAffinity-1, Japan). Uv–vis absorption spectra (Cary 50, Varian Company, USA). Fluorescence spectra (Cary 300, Varian Company, USA). Fluorescence microscope (LEICA DMI8). Leica biological microscope (LSCM, LEICA DM6000 B, Germany).

The reported methods were improved for preparation NCQDs [27,28]. The process is shown in Scheme 1 . 0.95 mL Ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), 0.90 g urea (CH4N2O), 1.80 g melamine (C3H6N6) and 0.55 mL ammonia solution (NH3·H2O), respectively, and 1.00 g citric acid (C6H8O7) were constantly stirred and dissolved in ultra-pure water. These mixtures were transferred into four 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with constant heat temperature at 200 °C for 5.0 h, respectively. After the reaction, the kettles were naturally cooled to room temperature. Then the mixtures were collected by some pipettes and centrifuged for 30 min at 6000 rpm. The resulting yellowish-brown supernatant was dialyzed by dialysis bags (3500 Da) for 24 h. Ultra-pure water was changed every eight hours. The obtained NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS) solutions were stored in a cool and dry place.

At room temperature, 2.00 mL the solutions of NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS) were added to four tubes, 2.00 mL Ag+ ions (700 μM) were added the above solutions, respectively, mixing constantly for 30 min. And then, 2.00 mL glucose (3500 μM) was added to the above tubes of mixed solution, respectively. The pH of the mixed solution was maintained at 7.5 by phosphate buffer saline (PBS). Then, all prepared samples were conducted by water-bath heating (60 °C) for 10 min and then cooled to room temperature. Then the fluorescence spectra were recorded upon excitations at 340 nm, 320 nm, 320 nm and 340 nm, respectively, in the wavelength range from 300 nm to 650 nm. The excitation and emission slits were 5.0 nm. In addition, the selectivity and anti-interference of the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions for the detection of glucose were confirmed through introducing different interferents in real urine samples. The analysis method was the same as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose of detection in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The urine samples come from normal adults. The urine samples were treated by centrifuge at 5000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant liquor was dialyzed. Then, 2.0 mL different urine samples were mixed with 2.0 mL mixed solutions of Ag+-NCQDs(AS) and water-bath heating (60 °C) for 10 min and then cooled to room temperature. The concentration of glucose were detected according to the above methods. Upon excitation at 340 nm, the fluorescence intensities were recorded in the range from 300 nm to 650 nm.

The cytotoxicity of NCQDs(AS) was performed in HeLa cells by MTT [29]. Firstly, the HeLa (2 × 104) were sowed in 96-well plates, which were then incubated at 37 °C in incubators containing 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then the original culture media was displaced with different NCQDs(AS) concentrations (0–300 μM), and then the HeLa cells were cultivated for 24 h. After that, the cells in the plates were conducted by 10.0 μL MTT solution (5.0 mg/mL), and incubated for 4 h in the incubator. The medium containing MTT was putting away and then washed with DMSO. Finally, cell survival was photographed at 490 nm using the fluorescence microscope (LEICA DMI8).

The HeLa cells (2 × 104) were placed in confocal culture dishes and cultured in DMEM medium at 37 °C in an incubator containing 5% CO2 for 24 h [30,31]. On the second day, after the cells were attached to the surface, the original culture media was displaced with 500 μL fresh samples NCQDs(AS) (250 μM), and then the HeLa cells were cultivated for another 8 h. Then the cultured cells were irrigated three times with PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.50), removing the excess NCQDs(AS). Then the cells were immobilized with 4% polyoxymethylene for 15 min, and irrigated three times with PBS. The cells were immersed in the film containing DAPI. Upon excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm, the cultured cells were photographed by using the confocal microscopy. Shoot under oil lens (630X).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, FT-IR and XPS of prepared NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and particle sizes of the NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) were recorded by TEM and HR-TEM images (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1(a-1), the NCQDs(EDA) nano-particles are uniformly dispersed, and the average particle size is about 4.81 nm in the range of 3.15–7.11 nm (Fig. 1(a-3)). In Fig. 1(a-2)), the lattice spacings of NCQDs(EDA) are about 0.34 nm and 0.36 nm in keeping with the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon [21,32]. As shown in Fig. 1(b-1), the prepared NCQDs(U) are nearly spherical nanoparticles with a average particle size of 2.88 nm (Fig. 1(b-3)). The obtained lattice spacings of NCQDs(U) by HR-TEM are approximately 0.27 nm and 0.34 nm (Fig. 1(b-2)) in accordance with the (100) and (002) crystal surfaces of graphite carbon, respectively [30]. The morphology and particle size of NCQDs(MA) are shown in Fig. 1(c). It can be found that the small particles of the NCQDs(MA) show a good distribution in the solution with an average grain diameter of 3.27 nm within the scope of 2.36–3.98 nm (Fig. 1(c-3)). The HR-TEM image in Fig. 1(c-2) reveals that the lattice fringes of NCQDs(MA) are about 0.22 nm and 0.23 nm in line with the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon [33]. As shown in Fig. 1(d-1), the NCQDs(AS) are regularly spherical nanoparticles. Particle size distribution is concentrated in the range from 2.21 nm to 3.70 nm with an average diameter of 2.80 nm (Fig. 1(d-3)). The HR-TEM image of NCQDs(AS) shows the lattice fringes of 0.22 nm and 0.23 nm, which are consistent with the (100) plane of graphite carbon [33]. In summary, the synthesized NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) have the characteristics of graphite structure, and the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are obtained.

The functional groups on the surfaces of NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) were investigated by FT-IR. As can be seen from Fig. 2 (a), a broad characteristic peak at around 3428 cm−1 are observed, which can be attributed to the O–H/N–H stretching vibration of amino and hydroxyl groups and the peak at 3253 cm−1 can be ascribed to the N–H stretching vibration [34]. It indicates that the ethylenediamine (EDA) as nitrogen sources have been successfully doped into the NCQDs [35]. The stretching vibration peak of the –CO in amide appears at around 1654 cm−1 [36], and the peak located at around 1568 cm−1 due to the CC stretching vibration. It suggests that CC unsaturated bonds have been formed in NCQDs(EDA), which agree well with the results for the lattice spacing from HR-TEM [37]. Whereas the characteristic absorption band at 1385 cm−1 is the flexural vibration of C–NH [33,34,38]. The small peak at 1127 cm−1 is associated with the C–O stretching vibration [39]. And the bending vibration of N–H is located at 607 cm−1 [33,36]. In addition, similar characteristic peaks can be also observed for NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) in Fig. 2(b–d), respectively. Therefore, these results confirm that the surfaces of synthesized NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) contain amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. It also demonstrate that these NCQDs possess good water solubility, high affinity to ions and excellent optical properties [35].

The elemental composition of NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) were researched by XPS (Fig. 3 ). The XPS survey spectrum of NCQDs(EDA) is shown in Fig. 3(a-1), the three peaks at 285.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 532.08 eV are exhibited, which principally correspond to C1s (78.33%), N1s (5.24%) and O1s (16.24%), respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 3(b-1), the prepared NCQDs(U) are mainly made up of C1s (73.91%), N1s (3.64%) and O1s (22.45%) with the binding energy of 284.80 eV, 399.80 eV, and 531.80 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(c-1), the XPS survey spectrum of NCQDs(MA) displays the presence of C1s, N1s and O1s, which mainly correspond to C1s (75.03%), N1s (3.41%) and O1s (21.56%), respectively. The full scan XPS spectrum in Fig. 3(d-1) declares that the NCQDs(AS) contain C, N and O elements, and the atomic concentration (%) of three elements are 79.25%, 1.47% and 19.28%, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S4(a-2), the C1s (284.8 eV) spectrum shows four peaks at 284.1 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.4 eV, which are assigned to the C–C/CC, C-N, C-OH and CO, respectively [33,37,40]. Similar detection results are obtained by analyzing the C1s HR-XPS of NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) from Fig. S4(b-2), (c-2) and (d-2), respectively, suggesting that the four kinds NCQDs have analogous chemical construction. In addition, in Fig. S4(a-3), the peaks at 399.5 eV (C–N) and 400.3 eV (N–H) are corresponding to the the N1s (399.5 eV) spectrum [34,37]. And the O1s (532.5 eV) spectrum is also deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.5 eV (CO) and 532.9 eV (C–OH) in Fig. S4(a-4) [21,41]. Similar results can be also observed by analyzing the N1s and O1s HR-XPS of NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) from Fig. S4(b-3, b-4), (c-3, c-4) and (d-3, d-4).

To sum up all, for NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS), they all consist of C, N and O elements. It further indicates that the nitrogen atoms have been successfully doped into the NCQDs, which is consistent with the FT-IR data. However, the doped nitrogen atoms concentration are different for NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS), which may affect the coordination ability with Ag+ ions. Only a certain amount of nitrogen atoms are doped into the NCQDs, which is beneficial to the coordination with Ag+ ions on the surface of NCQDs.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) in aqueous solutions were shown in Fig. 4 . As can be seen from Fig. 4(a–d), the NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) in aqueous solutions give absorption peaks at 245 nm, 250 nm, 235 nm and 240 nm, respectively, which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC [39]. In addition, the NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) in aqueous solutions also display distinct absorption peaks at 340 nm, 329 nm, 318 nm and 332 nm, respectively, which could belong to the n-π* transition of CO and N–H [42]. As shown in Fig. S5 and Table S2, with quinine sulfate as the reference (QY of quinine sulfate is 54%), the excitation wavelength is 340 nm, the QYs of NCQDs(EDA) and NCQDs(AS) are 38.2% and 19.4%. The excitation wavelength is 320 nm, the QYs of NCQDs(U) and NCQDs(MA) are 24.9% and 20.5%.

As shown in Fig. 4(a), with the increase of excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 340 nm, the emission peaks intensity of of NCQDs(EDA) are gradually strengthened. When the excitation wavelength is 340 nm, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs(EDA) at 450 nm reaches the maximum. Then, with the further increase of excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 380 nm, the fluorescence intensity begins to weaken, accompanied by a certain red shift. Similar phenomenons can be seen from Fig. 4(b–d). In summary, the optimal excitation wavelengths of NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS) are 340 nm, 320 nm, 320 nm and 340 nm, respectively, the corresponding maximum emission peaks locate at 445 nm, 418 nm, 415 nm and 441 nm, respectively.

The fluorescence of NCQDs can be attributable to the nitrogen doping on the surface of NCQDs. Nitrogen doping can cause a new surface state, which facilitate electron capture. It will enhance the yield of radiation recombination [43,44]. Fig. 4e and Table S1 presents the fluorescence decay profiles of NCQDs(EDA) and NCQDs(AS) by monitoring emission at 490 nm and 482 nm, respectively, with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. And the fluorescence decay profiles of NCQDs(U) and NCQDs(MA) by monitoring emission at 489 nm and 470 nm, respectively, with the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The average fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS) are 10.81 ns, 9.68 ns, 10.28 ns and 8.06 ns, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of prepared NCQDs in the presence of Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectra of NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) in aqueous solutions in the presence of Ag+ ions were measured, respectively. In addition, their fluorescence spectra after adding glucose were also measured, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 5 ((a-1)-(d-1)), the NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) in aqueous solution display distinct absorption peaks at 340 nm, 329 nm, 318 nm and 332 nm, respectively, which belong to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of CO and CC [42]. Among them, the absorption peak intensity of NCQDs(AS) at 332 nm is the most significant. It is because the individual nitrogen atoms of ammonia are more likely to enter carbon quantum dots and bond with carbon atoms. After Ag+ ions are added to NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solutions, respectively, the absorption peak intensities at 340 nm, 329 nm, 318 nm and 332 nm are all weakened. Because the coordination reaction happened between the –NH2 on the surface of NCQDs and Ag+ ions, the available adsorption sites are reduced, which causes the absorption peak intensity to be weakened. However, the decrease of absorption peak intensity of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution at 332 nm is most significant after adding Ag+ ions. In the presence of glucose, the absorption peak intensity of Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution is significantly restored. Therefore, the NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescent probe in the presence of Ag+ ions have great potential to detect glucose.

In addition, in Fig. 5((a-2)-(d-2)), upon excitations at 340 nm, 320 nm, 320 nm and 340 nm, respectively, the NCQDs(EDA) (a), NCQDs(U) (b), NCQDs(MA) (c) and NCQDs(AS) (d) in aqueous solutions at 445 nm, 418 nm, 415 nm and 441 nm all emit strong fluorescence. Nevertheless, after adding Ag+ ions, the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs(EDA), NCQDs(U), NCQDs(MA) and NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solutions are all quenched obviously along with a small red shift due to the surface energy transfer from NCQDs to Ag+ ions. The fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution is quenched most distinctly, compared with those of other three NCQDs. However, the fluorescence intensity of Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution is significantly enhanced or the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) is remarkably restored in the presence of glucose. And that, in the presence of glucose, the fluorescence of Ag+-NCQDs(EDA), Ag+-NCQDs(U) and Ag+-NCQDs(MA) solution are hardly recovered.

It is because that strong coordination bonds can be formed between excessive –NH2 on the surface of NCQDs and Ag+ ions. In the presence of glucose, Ag+ ions are not easy to break away from the surface of NCQDs under weak alkaline conditions, so that the fluorescence of NCQDs is difficult to be recovered. On the contrary, although the doped nitrogen atom concentration for NCQDs(AS) is relatively small, it can effectively coordinate with Ag+ ions. Under weak alkaline conditions, the glucose is oxidized and Ag+ ions are reduced, so that Ag+ ions deviate from the surface of the NCQDs(AS) and the fluorescence is restored. The experimental results are consistent with the XPS data. Therefore, the NCQDs(AS) is selected as a fluorescence probe to detect glucose in the presence of Ag+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH values, NaCl concentration and light irradiation time on the fluorescence of NCQDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the stability of NCQDs in aqueous solution, the influence of different pH values, NaCl concentration and light irradiation time on the fluorescence of NCQDs were researched and the results were shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S3. The influence of different pH values on the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 6(a), the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution at 441 nm displays weak fluorescence over the pH range 1.0 to 4.0. It is related to the protonation of the hydroxyl group on the surface of NCQDs(AS). The formation of hydrogen bonding results in the aggregation of NCQDs(AS), and the fluorescence system is destroyed. With the increase of pH value in the range from 5.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution are significantly heighten. When the pH is 7.0, the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum, and then the fluorescence intensities are only tiny weakened even if the pH values continued to increase. It suggests that the NCQDs(AS) have a wide suitability range of pH values.

In Fig. 6(b), the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution are hardly affected with the increase of NaCl solution concentration from 0.1 to 4.0 mol/L. It indicates that the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution have great stability even if the NaCl solution concentration is 4.0 mol/L. Besides, the influence of light irradiation time on the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 6(c). The fluorescence intensities of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution are not influenced with the change of light irradiation time. In summary, the NCQDs(AS) have outstanding optical stability and can be used in the study of fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Ag+ ions concentration on the fluorescence spectrum of NCQDs(AS)
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different Ag+ ions concentration on the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution were studied and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 7 . As can be seen from Fig. 7(a), with the increasing Ag+ ions concentration, the fluorescent intensities of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution are gradually weakened. When the concentration of Ag+ ions reaches 700 μM, the functional groups on the surface of NCQDs(AS) bounding with Ag+ ions tend to be saturated, and the fluorescence intensities barely change even as the Ag+ ions concentration up to 3000 μM. A good linear relation between Ag+ ions concentration and F0/F ratio has been shown in Fig. 7(b), the linear equation is F0/F = 0.02651[Ag+] + 0.99433 (R2 = 0.98166), where F0 and F are fluorescence intensity of NCQDs(AS) solution in the absence and presence of Ag+ ions, respectively.

To compare the fluorescence changes of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence and absence of Ag+ ions, the images of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence and absence of Ag+ ions were respectively photographed under visible-light and ultraviolet-light, and the results were shown in Fig. 7(c). Under the visible-light, NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution exhibit brownish yellow and colorless in the absence and presence of Ag+ ions, respectively. Under the ultraviolet-light, the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution show bright blue fluorescence. Upon addition of Ag+ ions, the bright blue fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution is notably weakened. It indicates that the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution is quenched because of the addition of Ag+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution acidity and glucose concentration on fluorescence spectra of NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of Ag+ ions, the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution can oxidate glucose without using glucose oxidase under weakly alkaline condition. Glucose is oxidized to gluconic acid and Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag. And then Ag+ ions break away from the surface of NCQDs(AS), resulting the fluorescence recovery of NCQDs(AS).

As shown in Fig. 8 (a-1), upon excitation at 340 nm, the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution at 441 nm emits strong fluorescence. After adding Ag+ ions, the fluorescence of the NCQDs(AS) is obviously quenched. After a certain concentration glucose is added to the Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution, with the increase of pH value, the fluorescence intensities of the NCQDs(AS) are gradually enhanced. The fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs(AS) reaches maximum at pH = 7.5, and then the fluorescence intensities are weakened gradually even if the pH continues to increase. The results show that the fluorescence recovery of the NCQDs(AS) is closely related to the pH value. The more intuitive comparisons can be seen from Fig. 8(a-2), when the pH is 7.0, the fluorescence recovery of the NCQDs(AS) reaches the maximum. Under acidic or strongly alkaline conditions, the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution has hardly been recovered. The oxidation of glucose by Ag+ ions in the presence of NCQDs(AS) could not occur under acidic conditions. This is because, under acidic conditions, fristly, there are lots of H+ ions in the solution. Therefore, the NCQDs(AS) prefer to bond with H+ ions compared with Ag+ ions. Secondly, even if the coordination between the –NH2 on the surface of NCQDs(AS) and Ag+ ions was occurred, after adding the glucose, some glucose will cover the surface of the NCQDs(AS), causing the fluorescence of the NCQDs(AS) to be difficult to recover. Under strongly alkaline conditions, the glucose can not be oxidized by Ag+ ions even in the presence of NCQDs(AS). In fact, pH > 8.0 solution are no longer the optimal acidity condition for silver mirror reaction, and the oxidation capacity of Ag+ ions completely disappears at pH > 12. Therefore, the optimal condition for the detection of glucose is pH = 7.5.

The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions and different glucose concentration was studied under optimal conditions in Fig. 8(b-1). The fluorescence intensities of the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions are gradually enhanced with the increase of glucose concentration. When the concentration of glucose is 3500 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs(AS) reaches the maximum, and then the fluorescence intensities hardly change even if the glucose concentration continued to increase. It is because that the excess glucose is added to the Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution, the more Ag+ ions on the surface of NCQDs(AS) are reduced to Ag. The generated Ag precipitations will partially deposit on the surface of NCQDs(AS), preventing the complete fluorescence recovery of the NCQDs(AS). It shows the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions is strongly dependent on the glucose concentrations.

In addition, as shown in Fig. 8(b-2), a excellent linear relationships between the fluorescence recovery factors F/F0 and the glucose concentrations were obtained in the range from 0.1 μM to 1000 μM with a regression coefficient of 0.99291, where F and F0 were fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of glucose, respectively. And the low detection limit (LOD) of 0.375 μM (S/N = 3) is acquired. Compared with other methods (in Table 1 ), the prepared NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions has the wide detection range and low detection limit. Therefore, the non-enzymatic fluorescent probe based on the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions can be successfully applied for the quantitative analysis of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference of NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescence probe in the presence of Ag+ ions for the detection of glucose, the influences of some related coexistent matters, such as NaCl, KCl, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), orotic acid (OA), histidine (His), creatinine (Cre), Fe3+ and Cu2+ coexisting in real urine, were studied. The same concentration of coexistent matters (3500 μM) are added into the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions, respectively, and the experimental results were shown in Fig. 9 a. Upon excitation at 340 nm, after Ag+ ions were added to NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs(AS) at 441 nm was significantly quenched. After adding glucose, the fluorescence response of NCQDs(AS) at 441 nm (λex = 340 nm) significantly enhanced, and little effect on the fluorescence responses of NCQDs(AS) were observed for the addition of other coexistent matters. The corresponding histogram was shown in Fig. 9b. After other coexistent matters were added to the Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution, respectively, all fluorescence responses of the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions barely changed. However, only after adding glucose, the fluorescence response of the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions is obviously enhanced. These results demonstrate that the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution as a fluorescent probe in the presence of Ag+ ions can be used to detect glucose specifically.

Furthermore, with regard to some interferents those widely coexist with glucose in urine, the anti-interference ability of NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescence probe in the presence of Ag+ ions was also investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 10 a, upon excitation at 340 nm, the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution emitted strong fluorescence at 441 nm. After adding Ag+ ions, the fluorescence response of the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution was significantly quenched. And then, the fluorescence of the NCQDs(AS) was remarkably restored after the glucose was added to the Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution. However, the fluorescence responses of NCQDs(AS) were hardly changed after adding other interferents. It indicates that the NCQDs(AS) in the presence of Ag+ ions displays strong anti-interference ability for the detection of glucose even in the presence of interferents. The more intuitive comparison was shown in Fig. 10b. In the presence of Ag+ ions, the fluorescence of the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution was obviously quenched. After adding the glucose to the Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution, the fluorescence was significantly enhanced or the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) was remarkably restored. However, after adding other interferents, for the fluorescence of the Ag+-NCQDs(AS) solution in the presence of the glucose, no significant change happens. Obviously, the NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescent probe in the presence of Ag+ ions has strong anti-interference ability in detecting glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and mechanism of glucose detection by using NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescent probe in the presence of Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The description of the mechanism on glucose detection by using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs(AS)) from ammonia solution (AS) as nitrogen source in the presence of Ag+ ions is shown in Fig. 11 . It is well known that the photoluminescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is caused by the holes on the surface of the CQDs, in which the holes store energy. The fluorescence of CQDs can be quenched by adding some metal ions due to the energy transfer from CQDs to these metal ions. Upon excitation at 340 nm, the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution emits strong fluorescence at 441 nm. After Ag+ ions were added to the NCQDs(AS) solution, the fluorescence of the NCQDs(AS) solution was significantly quenched. It is because that the surface of NCQDs(AS) have abundant amines, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which have good affinity to coordinate with Ag+ ions, facilitating energy transfer from the NCQDs(AS) to Ag+ ions.

Under weakly alkaline condition, the Ag+-(NCQDs-NH2)2-OH in aqueous solution, which is similar to the silver ammonia [Ag(NH3)2]OH solution, were formed through the coordination between the –NH2 on the surface of NCQDs(AS) and Ag+ ions. Even without glucose oxdiase, the glucose can also be oxidized to gluconic acid and Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag. Then, Ag+ ions are removed from the surface of NCQDs(AS), resulting in the recovery of fluorescence of NCQDs(AS). Therefore, the NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution as a fluorescence probe in the presence of Ag+ ions can be applied to detect glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose detection in real sample analyses
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescence probe in the presence of Ag+ ions for the glucose detection in real urine samples, the comparisons with different methods were implemented and the obtained results were listed in Table 2 . The different concentrations of glucose in real urine samples were calculated according to the calibration equation mentioned above. In addition,

to demonstrate the accuracy of our proposed method, the real urine samples were also determined by glucometer and HPLC. The results indicate that the glucose concentrations in real urine samples agree well with the values obtained from glucometer and HPLC, which verify the accuracy of the proposed method. In addition, the relative standard deviations (% RSD) are lower than 4.0%. It shows the proposed method has relatively good precision. The experimental results indicate that the NCQDs(AS) as a fluorescence probe in the presence of Ag+ ions have potential applicability in the quantitative glucose detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging of NCQDs(AS)
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of NCQDs(AS) was performed with HeLa cells by using MTT test. The cells were cultured in an incubator for 24 h under different NCQDs(AS) concentrations (0–300 μM). As can be seen from Fig. 12 , when the NCQDs(AS) concentration reaches to 250 μM, the cell viability is dropped to approximately 88%. It indicates that NCQDs(AS) has lower toxicity and good biocompatibility.

Subsequently, the confocal fluorescence images of the NCQDs(AS) with HeLa cells was researched. In Fig. 13 , upon excitation at 405 and 488 nm, the HeLa cells that were cultured with NCQDs(AS) emitted bright fluorescence. Without the interference of DAPI, upon excitation at 488 nm, the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) was mainly concentrated in the cytoplasm, and the nucleus and membrane basically did not exist. It further demonstrates that the fluorescent probe NCQDs(AS) possesses low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Therefore, the NCQDs(AS) can be used as a good bioimaging material in practical application.

In summary, four kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were prepared by one step hydrothermal method. Among them, the NCQDs(AS) from ammonia solution (AS) as nitrogen source are uniform size spherical nanoparticles and have prominent optical properties. In the presence of Ag+ ions, the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution is quenched obviously due to the energy transfer from NCQDs(AS) to Ag+ ions. Under weakly alkaline condition (pH = 7.5), the detected glucose is oxidized to gluconic acid and Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag in aqueous solution at 60 °C. Because the Ag+ ions break away from the surface of NCQDs(AS), the fluorescence of NCQDs(AS) is recovered. The NCQDs(AS) in aqueous solution in the presence of Ag+ ions exhibits excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability with a wide detection range (0.1 μM–1000 μM) and low detection limit (LOD) of 0.375 μM in the non-enzymatic detection of glucose. It also gives good results in real sample detection. In addition, the NCQDs(AS) reveals low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Based on the high sensitivity, wide linear region, low detection limit and low cost, in the presence of Ag+ ions, the NCQDs(AS) as a non-enzyme fluorescent probe is expected to be used in the glucose detection in medical examination and clinical diagnosis.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

Wenfang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Haishuang Jia: Investigation. Jie Zhang: Investigation. Jianhe Tang: Investigation. Jun Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources. Dawei Fang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (21371084), and the Innovative Team Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province (LT2018018) of China. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participating in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105187.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Mater. 24 (2012) 2037-2041.
